title: Quick check: check newly enrolled MFA devices

questions.prompt: Why is the MFA-method audit a separate step after the password and MFA reset?
choices: Some identity providers preserve method history through resets.

choices.label: Because Huntress requires it by policy.
choices.rationale: Not a documented policy.

choices.label: Because MFA methods cannot be removed during the reset step.
choices.rationale: Wrong on platform behaviour.

choices.label: Because the SOC always sets a separate timer on MFA review.
choices.rationale: Not a real platform behaviour.

questions.explanation: Or the reset may have removed methods broadly when targeted removal was the intent. The audit reconciles. The reset prompts re-enrolment but does not always clean up the existing method list. Audit the post-reset state rather than assuming.

questions.prompt: Step 5 on Farah. Timeline: 08:42 "MFA method added: phone +66-..."; 08:44 "MFA method removed: phone +61-...". Current methods: authenticator app (18 months old); phone ending -7799 (registered 08:42). What do you do?

choices.label: Remove all methods (the -7799 phone and the authenticator app) to be safe.
choices.rationale: The authenticator app is 18 months old. Clearly legitimate. Removing it forces unnecessary re-enrolment.

choices: Remove the phone ending -7799 (added in the compromise window).

choices.label: Remove the -7799 phone and re-add the +61- number yourself.
choices.rationale: You do not add MFA methods on the user's behalf. The user re-enrols their own.

questions.explanation: Leave the authenticator app. Brief Farah that the attacker removed her +61- phone and she will need to re-register if she wants SMS as backup. Targeted removal: remove the attacker-added method, preserve the legitimate one, brief the user on what is missing.

questions.prompt: Farah says: "I have an authenticator app from ages ago, yes. My phone is +61-, yes. And I think I also have an old work iPad enrolled from last year." What do you do?

choices.label: Believe Farah and do not check the Timeline.
choices.rationale: User recollection under stress is not reliable. Cross-reference with the Timeline.

choices: Verify each method against the Timeline.

choices.label: Tell Farah to sort her own MFA out and end the conversation.
choices.rationale: Wrong tone and substance. Customer comms is part of the close-out.

questions.explanation: Authenticator (18 months) confirmed legitimate; +61- phone (removed by attacker) confirmed hers; iPad: check whether any tablet-shaped method has a pre-compromise timestamp. Triangulate user recollection with the Timeline. Trust neither alone. The Timeline is the truth-source; the user is the corroboration.

questions.prompt: "Removing all MFA methods to be safe" has what specific cost?
choices: Forces complete re-enrolment the user may not have time for.

choices.label: There is no cost; it is the safe default.
choices.rationale: The lesson is explicit about the cost.

choices.label: All-method removal is not possible on M365.
choices.rationale: Wrong on platform behaviour.

choices.label: Triggers an automatic Huntress support ticket.
choices.rationale: Not a real platform behaviour.

questions.explanation: If they are not at their device, they cannot sign in until they get there. Operational cost: the user is locked out of MFA until they can re-enrol on their device. Targeted removal preserves what works.

questions.prompt: Trusting the user's verbal "those are all mine" without cross-referencing the Timeline is acceptable during a compromise response.
questions.explanation: A user under stress may not remember exactly which methods they registered. The Timeline gives you the truth. Cross-reference both.

questions.prompt: "Skipping this step because the reset in step 2 handled MFA" is wrong because:
choices: The reset prompts re-enrolment but does not always clean up the existing method list.

choices.label: Step 5 is optional and skipping is fine.
choices.rationale: It is the targeted-cleanup step.

choices.label: The reset always fails on MFA methods.
choices.rationale: Overstates. The reset usually works; the audit handles the edge cases.

choices.label: The step is only relevant for admin accounts.
choices.rationale: Wrong on scope. It applies to all affected identities.

questions.explanation: Some identity providers retain method history through a reset. Audit rather than assume. Trust-but-verify the post-reset state. Method history retention through resets is a real platform behaviour on some identity providers.

questions.prompt: The user has a method they cannot identify, and the Timeline does not conclusively map it to the attacker. What do you do?
choices: Bump for senior review of the user's full MFA history.

choices.label: Remove it as a precaution.
choices.rationale: Could be legitimate. Targeted-removal discipline says do not act without identification.

choices.label: Leave it because the Timeline does not show it as attacker-added.
choices.rationale: Ambiguity warrants escalation, not default-leave.

choices.label: Open a Huntress support ticket.
choices.rationale: Senior is the escalation path.

questions.explanation: Could be a legitimate method they forgot about; could be attacker-added from a prior compromise. Ambiguous-method patterns escalate. The senior reviews the user's full MFA history for context.

questions.prompt: Multiple users in the same tenant have attacker-added MFA methods in the same window. What do you do?

choices.label: Run the playbook on each user in sequence.
choices.rationale: Solo per-user playbook on a tenant-wide event lets the attacker continue moving.

choices.label: Treat as coincidence; each user separately.
choices.rationale: The cross-user pattern is the signal.

choices.label: Wait to see if more users are affected before deciding.
choices.rationale: Two users in the same window is already the pattern.

questions.explanation: Multi-user MFA-tampering pattern is a tenant-wide signal. Bump to senior.

questions.prompt: The user's only legitimate MFA method has been removed by the attacker. They have no way to satisfy MFA on next sign-in. What do you do?

choices.label: Set up a new MFA method for the user yourself.
choices.rationale: You do not add MFA methods on the user's behalf.

choices.label: Disable MFA for the user temporarily.
choices.rationale: Disables protection during a compromise response. Senior call at best.

choices: Bump. The recovery path is a senior-assisted re-enrolment.

choices.label: Tell the user to wait 24 hours until methods regenerate.
choices.rationale: Methods do not regenerate. Wrong on platform behaviour.

questions.explanation: When the user is locked out of MFA entirely, the senior owns the recovery path.

items: List the user's current MFA methods.
Map each method to the Timeline (compromise-window additions vs pre-existing).
Identify and remove attacker-added methods (targeted removal).
Brief the user on re-enrolment if their legitimate methods were removed.
Verify the list reflects only legitimate methods.
questions.explanation: List, map to Timeline, targeted remove, brief user, verify. The Timeline-mapping step is what separates targeted from blanket removal.
